title: Walking an Array
lesson:
This is the program that ties RISC together. To process a list, you keep a pointer in a register and walk it forward, four bytes at a time (since each word is 32 bits = 4 bytes). 'lw' reads through the pointer, you add the element to a running sum, 'addi' bumps the pointer, and 'bne' loops while the counter is non-zero.

This is the exact shape of every for-loop over an array in every C program you'll ever read. Compilers turn `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) sum += a[i];` into almost exactly this MIPS code. When you meet the same idea in LC-3 (LDR) and ARM (LDR), you'll see it's the same routine wearing different clothes.
prompt: Sum the five numbers in the array (10, 20, 3, 7, 5 → 45) using a pointer + lw + bne loop. Print the result with syscall 1.
starterCode:
.data
arr:    .word 10, 20, 3, 7, 5
.text
        la   $t0, arr          # pointer to first element
        li   $t1, 5            # count
        li   $t2, 0            # sum
loop:
        lw   $t3, 0($t0)       # load element
        add  $t2, $t2, $t3     # sum += element
        addi $t0, $t0, 4       # advance pointer by 4 bytes (one word)
        addi $t1, $t1, -1
        bne  $t1, $zero, loop
        move $a0, $t2
        li   $v0, 1
        syscall
        li   $v0, 10
        syscall
hints: la $t0, arr puts the address of the first element into $t0.
lw $t3, 0($t0) loads the element the pointer is on; addi $t0, $t0, 4 advances to the next.
Loop with bne $t1, $zero, loop using $t1 as the count. After the loop, print the sum (45).
successText: The console must print 45 (the sum of the array).
reference:
.data
arr: .word 10, 20, 3, 7, 5
.text
la $t0, arr
li $t1, 5
li $t2, 0
loop:
lw $t3, 0($t0)
add $t2, $t2, $t3
addi $t0, $t0, 4
addi $t1, $t1, -1
bne $t1, $zero, loop
move $a0, $t2
li $v0, 1
syscall
li $v0, 10
syscall
